2	// AG GRID MODULE REGISTRATION — SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH
3	// =============================================================================
4	//
5	// ⚠️ READ THIS BEFORE ADDING OR CHANGING ANY AG GRID FEATURE ⚠️
6	//
7	// AG Grid (v33+, this repo is on 36.x) only enables a feature if its MODULE is
8	// registered here. This file is the ONE place the app registers AG Grid modules.
9	//
10	// TODAY: we register `AllEnterpriseModule` — every Community + Enterprise feature
11	// is available, so nothing can be "missing". This is intentional and safe.
12	//
13	// IF YOU EVER TRIM TO A HAND-PICKED MODULE LIST (to shrink the bundle):
14	//   1. Replace `AllEnterpriseModule` below with the explicit modules you use
15	//      (e.g. ClientSideRowModelModule, ServerSideRowModelModule, MasterDetailModule,
16	//      SetFilterModule, ContextMenuModule, ColumnMenuModule, ClipboardModule,
17	//      CsvExportModule, ExcelExportModule, RangeSelectionModule, RowGroupingModule,
18	//      StatusBarModule, SideBarModule, …). Use AG Grid's "Module Selector" to derive
19	//      the list from the features in use.
20	//   2. **If you add ANY new AG Grid Enterprise feature anywhere in the app
21	//      (a colDef option, a gridOptions flag, a context-menu item, an export call,
22	//      a filter type, master/detail, row grouping, status bar, side bar, etc.)
23	//      you MUST add its module to the list below — otherwise the feature will
24	//      silently no-op in production.**
25	//   3. Keep development validations enabled in NON-production builds. In v36,
26	//      ValidationModule before any grid is created instead. It logs a console
27	//      warning/error naming missing modules — the safety net that turns a silent
28	//      prod failure into a loud dev one.
29	//   4. Do NOT trim without the enforcement gates (a test that fails on AG Grid
30	//      console errors + a per-grid feature smoke test). See
31	//      `docs/perf-developer-guide.md` (point #6) and `HANDOFF.md`.
32	//
33	// Registration must run before ANY grid is instantiated; importing this file for
34	// its side effect from the root AppModule guarantees that.
35	// =============================================================================
36	
37	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
38	// CANDIDATE hand-picked list (audited 2026-06-22 — NOT yet active)
39	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
40	// A grep audit of every grid page found these FEATURES in use (high confidence):
41	//   • Row models: client-side (RFQ) + server-side (itemLibrary, prod_tracking, licensing)
42	//   • Master/Detail, Cell selection (range), Clipboard, Excel + CSV export
43	//   • Filters: Set, Multi, Text, Number, Date  (+ custom Angular filters)
44	//   • Side bar + Columns/Filters tool panels, Status bar (agg/total/selected counts)
45	//   • Context menu, Column menu, pagination, tooltips, agSelectCellEditor,
46	//     custom Angular cell renderers/editors
47	//   • colDefs also contain rowGroup / enablePivot / groupDisplayType / aggFunc
48	//     → VERIFY whether grouping/pivot is actually exercised at runtime before relying on it.
49	//
50	// The corresponding v36 module list is roughly:
51	//   ClientSideRowModelModule, ServerSideRowModelModule, MasterDetailModule,
52	//   CellSelectionModule, ClipboardModule, CsvExportModule, ExcelExportModule,
53	//   TextFilterModule, NumberFilterModule, DateFilterModule, SetFilterModule,
54	//   MultiFilterModule, CustomFilterModule, ColumnMenuModule, ContextMenuModule,
55	//   SideBarModule, ColumnsToolPanelModule, FiltersToolPanelModule, StatusBarModule,
56	//   PaginationModule, TooltipModule, RowGroupingModule, PivotModule,
57	//   CustomEditorModule, plus the many v33-split community API/style modules
58	//   (CellStyleModule, RowStyleModule, RowAutoHeightModule, ColumnAutoSizeModule,
59	//   RenderApiModule, RowApiModule, ColumnApiModule, EventApiModule, …) and
60	//   `ValidationModule` in non-prod.
61	//
62	// ⚠️ This is a CANDIDATE, not a guarantee. Deriving the exact, COMPLETE v35 module
63	// set by inspection is the error-prone step (a single miss = silent prod no-op).
64	// The reliable way to finalize it: switch to ValidationModule + this list in a dev
65	// build and iterate on AG Grid's console errors until clean, then lock it with a
66	// per-grid feature smoke test (see docs/perf-developer-guide.md #6). Until that is
67	// done, we deliberately keep the all-features bundle below.
68	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
69	
70	import { AllEnterpriseModule, ModuleRegistry } from 'ag-grid-enterprise';
71	import { ValidationModule, provideGlobalGridOptions } from 'ag-grid-community';
72	import { environment } from '../../../environments/environment';
73	
74	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
75	// THEMING — keep the legacy CSS themes (`ag-theme-alpine` + the imported
76	// ag-grid.css / ag-theme-alpine.css in styles.scss / angular.json).
77	//
78	// AG Grid v33+ made the new Theming API the DEFAULT. When a grid does NOT set a
79	// `theme`, v36 applies the Theming API and it COLLIDES with our legacy CSS —
80	// the rows load but render invisibly (blank grid). None of our grids set a
81	// `theme`, so this broke every grid app-wide after the v36 upgrade.
82	//
83	// `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' })` sets the default for ALL grids
84	// created afterward, so they keep using the legacy CSS themes. A grid can still
85	// opt into the new Theming API by setting its own `theme` (e.g. a themeQuartz
86	// object) in its grid options — that per-grid value overrides this default.
87	// Must run before any grid is instantiated; this file already guarantees that.
88	// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
89	provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' });
90	
91	/**
92	 * The exact module set registered with AG Grid. Exported so tests / diagnostics
93	 * can assert what is registered (see `ModuleRegistry.isRegistered(...)` /
94	 * `gridApi.isModuleRegistered(...)`). When trimming, this array becomes the
95	 * explicit allow-list instead of the all-features bundle.
96	 */
97	export const REGISTERED_AG_GRID_MODULES = [AllEnterpriseModule];
98	
99	if (!environment.production) {
100	  ModuleRegistry.registerModules([ValidationModule]);
101	}
102	
103	ModuleRegistry.registerModules(REGISTERED_AG_GRID_MODULES);

content.text:
Verified. The code confirms `fix_theming_api.md` is the accurate one. The live app is **on legacy CSS**, not the new Theming API. Three places prove it:

1. **`ag-grid-modules.ts:89`** — `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' })` sets *every* grid to legacy by default. Its header comment (lines 74–88) spells out exactly why: v36 made the Theming API the default, it collided with the loaded legacy CSS, and every grid went blank — so they pinned to legacy.

2. **`grid-ui.service.ts:38`** — `bootstrapTheme()` actively forces `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', 'legacy')` on each grid, and the comment notes the toolbar theme picker is **inert** as a result.

3. **`styles.scss:8–9`** — the legacy CSS files are still imported:
   ```scss
   @use "ag-grid-community/styles/ag-grid.css";
   @use "ag-grid-community/styles/ag-theme-alpine.css";
   ```

## Bottom line

| Doc | Claim | Reality |
|---|---|---|
| `fix_theming_api.md` | App is on **legacy CSS**; Theming API switch is planned, not started | ✅ **Correct** |
| `docs/ag-grid-36-migration-plan.md` | "No grid uses `theme: 'legacy'`" / Theming API already done | ❌ **Stale/wrong** |

So in plain terms: the actual switch from legacy CSS to the new Theming API **has not happened**. The app was deliberately kept on legacy CSS to avoid the blank-grid problem, and `fix_theming_api.md` is the plan for doing the switch later. The line in the v36 migration doc claiming the Theming API is already in use is simply out of date — it was likely written before the blank-grid incident forced the revert back to legacy.

One small note: that contradiction in `ag-grid-36-migration-plan.md` (§2 line 51, "No grid uses `theme: 'legacy'`") is now actively misleading. Want me to add a one-line correction note to that doc pointing readers to `fix_theming_api.md` for the real current state?

content.text:
I've now checked the plan against the actual code. Here's my honest assessment.

## Verdict: Yes — it's genuinely comprehensive, and its facts check out

I verified the plan's specific claims against the live code, and they're accurate:

| Plan claims | Reality | ✓ |
|---|---|---|
| `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' })` is set | `ag-grid-modules.ts:89` — exactly | ✓ |
| `bootstrapTheme()` forces `theme:'legacy'`, picker inert | `grid-ui.service.ts:38` — exactly | ✓ |
| Legacy CSS imported in `styles.scss` **and** `angular.json` | both confirmed (`styles.scss:8–9`, `angular.json:50–51`) | ✓ |
| Theming-API infra already half-built | `GRID_THEME_CONFIG`, `themePink`, `GridThemeEventsService`, picker all exist | ✓ |
| App's own SCSS uses **zero** `var(--ag-*)` | confirmed — all 1,875 `var(--ag-*)` hits are in the *vendored* `@vex/styles/ag-grid/` tree, none in app code | ✓ |
| `themeQuartz` already has `buttonStyleQuartz` + `columnDropStyleBordered` parts | confirmed (`grid-themes.config.ts:15–17`) — so the v36 §3.3 gotcha is already handled | ✓ |

That last one is a nice bonus: the doc worried buttons/column-drop might render unstyled, but the config **already** adds those parts. So that risk is pre-closed.

The plan is unusually good for an LLM hand-off: it has the exact files, the revert commits for rollback, a phased order, a per-grid QA checklist, and the RFQ-specific danger notes. I could execute Phases 1–4 (the code) confidently from it alone.

## The real gaps — and they're not gaps the doc *can* close

The honest limitation isn't the doc's thoroughness; it's that the hardest part of this job **isn't code, it's a visual-design judgment that needs your eye**:

1. **The base-theme decision is unmade (Phase 0).** The doc deliberately leaves "`themeQuartz` (the documented intent) vs `themeAlpine` (closest to today's look)" open. That's the single biggest fork, and it's a *taste* call — what should the grids look like afterward? No document can answer that for me. If you want them to look **identical** to today, that's hours of `withParams` color/spacing matching. If you're fine with a clean Quartz look, it's much faster.

2. **No automated visual safety net.** The plan itself says the real gate is "eyeball each of 10 grids on the deployed sandbox," ~6 min per deploy cycle. So "a very good job" means an iterative loop of *me changing → you looking → me adjusting*. I can't self-verify pixels.

3. **Dark mode is hand-waved.** The doc notes `ag-theme-alpine-dark` exists but doesn't confirm whether dark mode is *actually used* anywhere live. I'd want to verify that before ripping the class out.

## My recommendation

The doc is more than enough for me to do the **code** well. To do the whole **switchover** well, I'd need one thing from you up front — a decision I shouldn't make alone:

**Do you want the migrated grids to (a) look pixel-for-pixel like they do today, or (b) just look clean and correct on the new Quartz theme, accepting minor cosmetic shifts?**

(a) is more work and more back-and-forth QA; (b) is much faster. Everything else in the plan I can drive. Want me to start with the Phase 0 spike on **Item Library** (smallest CSS surface) so you can eyeball one grid before I commit to all ten?

content: will keeping themeAlpine make the transition easier?

message.content: will keeping themeAlpine make the transition easier?

content.text:
Yes — and it's the single biggest lever for making the *visual* half easier. Here's the precise reasoning.

## Why `themeAlpine` helps

The app today renders on the **legacy `ag-theme-alpine` CSS**. The Theming API's `themeAlpine` is AG Grid's modern reimplementation of that *same Alpine design* — same general header look, row heights, borders, font feel. So if you pick `themeAlpine` as the base, the grids' default appearance starts out **as close as possible to what you see now**.

That directly shrinks **Phase 5 — the long pole** (the 1–2 days of "eyeball 10 grids and chase visual regressions"). The closer the default is to today, the fewer `withParams` tweaks I have to make and the less back-and-forth QA with you.

By contrast, `themeQuartz` is a *different* visual design (flatter, different spacing/headers). Choosing it means I'd have to rebuild the Alpine look via `withParams` to avoid everything "looking different" — more work, more eyeballing.

## What it does NOT change

Be clear-eyed about this: the base theme **only affects the visual long pole.** The structural code work is identical either way:

- Flipping the engine off `theme:'legacy'` (Phase 2) — same.
- Stripping the `ag-theme-alpine` class from ~18 files (Phase 3) — same.
- Reauthoring the custom `.ag-*` SCSS (Phase 4) — same.

So `themeAlpine` makes the transition **easier, not shorter in code** — it cuts QA/polish time, not the plumbing.

## Two honest caveats

1. **It's not pixel-identical.** The legacy Alpine CSS and the new `themeAlpine` differ in small details (some spacing, border colors, sort glyphs). It's *close*, not a perfect match — there'd still be minor polish.
2. **Your brand customizations aren't part of either base theme.** Whatever the redesign layered on top (the `.ag-theme-alpine`-targeted rules in `styles.scss` and the per-page SCSS) still has to be re-expressed regardless. `themeAlpine` just means the *starting canvas* underneath those tweaks already looks right.

One more thing worth knowing: `AGENTS.md` §11 documented the *intent* as Quartz, and the existing custom `themePink` was built on a Quartz base. Picking Alpine diverges from that documented intent — totally fine (the plan says to update §11 anyway), just flagging it so you're choosing it knowingly.

## Bottom line

If your goal is "switch to the Theming API with the **least visual disruption and the smoothest QA**," then **yes, `themeAlpine` is the right base** — it's the path of least resistance. Pick Quartz only if you actually *want* the grids to look different/more modern.

Want me to start the Phase 0 spike on **Item Library** using `themeAlpine` as the base, so you can see one real grid side-by-side with today's look before I commit to all ten?

Find ag-grid CSS to theming API migration docs

content.text:
This is the decisive finding, and it changes one assumption in the plan. Let me explain plainly, because it affects what you'll see.

## What the AG Grid docs just confirmed

The docs are explicit about migrating when you have multiple grids:

> *"In order to migrate **one grid** on a page that contains multiple grids, you can use **shadow DOM** to isolate grids using v32 styles from grids using the Theming API. Be aware however that this is an **advanced technique**..."*

Here's the catch specific to your app: the legacy CSS (`ag-grid.css` + `ag-theme-alpine.css`) is loaded **globally** (in `styles.scss` and `angular.json`), so it sits on *every* page. The moment any single grid switches to the new Theming API while that global CSS is present, AG Grid throws **error #106** and the grid can render blank — exactly the original bug.

**So a truly isolated one-grid spike isn't cleanly possible** without an advanced Shadow-DOM hack that's risky and wouldn't even represent the real end state (grid popups/tooltips escape the shadow boundary and lose styling). I don't recommend it.

## What this means for the spike

The clean, representative way to spike Item Library is to **flip the engine globally** to `themeAlpine` (remove the global legacy CSS, point the default + `bootstrapTheme()` at `themeAlpine`) — and then **polish only Item Library's custom CSS** in this first pass.

The consequence: on the **sandbox**, *all* grids move to the new Alpine theme at once. Item Library will look properly polished (its CSS reauthored); the **other 9 grids will render fine but look "raw/unpolished"** until I reauthor their CSS in later passes. Nothing breaks functionally, it's only on `sandbox-albert`, and it's fully reversible by reverting the commit.

That's a bigger visual blast radius than the "just one grid" mental model — so I want your explicit OK before I touch the global engine.

questions.question: Since one grid can't be cleanly isolated, how do you want me to run the Item Library spike?

options.label: Global flip, polish Item Library
options.description: Switch the whole app's engine to themeAlpine now and fully polish only Item Library. On the sandbox, the other 9 grids render fine but look unpolished until I do their CSS in later passes. Clean, representative, reversible. Recommended.

options.label: Shadow-DOM isolate Item Library
options.description: Keep the other 9 grids pristine on legacy CSS and isolate only Item Library via Shadow DOM. Advanced/risky, and popups/tooltips may render unstyled — not representative of the real end state.

options.label: Skip spike, migrate all 10 now

content.text:
Got it — **full migration of all 10 grids in one pass**, no spike. That's a substantial change across ~35 files, so I'll work methodically and keep you posted.

Two process notes first:
- I'll **work locally and not commit** until you've reviewed — especially since the tree already has other uncommitted work I don't want to entangle.
- I'm **skipping the usual `develop` sync** (fetch/merge) because the working tree is dirty with that other work; merging on top of it risks conflicts and the kind of cross-session tangle your memory flags. I'll flag if that becomes a problem.

Let me build a complete, precise inventory of every `ag-theme-alpine` usage in app code (excluding the vendored theme tree I'll delete later) so nothing is missed.

aiTitle: Find ag-grid CSS to theming API migration docs

content.text:
Complete inventory captured. This actually reveals a **much safer approach** than the plan assumed — and it's explicitly endorsed by the AG Grid docs I just read:

> *"It is **not required** to remove the theme classname from your grid container."*

Every grid sets its theme via an `ag-theme-alpine` **class**, and all ~17 SCSS files scope their custom rules under `.ag-theme-alpine .ag-...`. If I **keep that class as a plain styling hook** (rather than ripping it out of 20 files), then once the legacy CSS is no longer *loaded*, `ag-theme-alpine` stops being an AG Grid theme and becomes just an app class — and **all the existing custom CSS keeps matching, unchanged.** The new `themeAlpine` provides the base look underneath.

That means the migration collapses to: flip the engine, stop loading the legacy CSS, keep the classes. Far less risk, and the other 9 grids keep their styling instead of going "raw."

The one thing that could break with this shortcut is **dark mode** (the `-dark` class won't theme anymore). Let me verify whether dark mode is actually reachable before I rely on it, and read the two global style blocks.

content.content:

304:    this.darkMode = backgroundCard;

content.text: Dark mode **is** conditionally wired — `gridClass` flips to `ag-theme-alpine-dark` when the app's `--background-card` variable is the dark value (`#1a202e`). So I can't ignore it without checking whether dark mode is actually reachable in the live app. Let me check the app's theme config.

aiTitle: Find ag-grid CSS to theming API migration docs

content.text:
Now I have what I need to make a safe decision. Here's the key finding on dark mode, then I'll execute.

**Dark mode reality:** The app *does* have a Vex dark-mode toggle, but the redesign's grid CSS is **already light-only** — every custom rule is scoped to `.ag-theme-alpine`, never `.ag-theme-alpine-dark`. So even today, switching to dark mode strips all the redesign styling off the grids. I'm therefore not regressing anything real. I'll keep light mode pixel-correct (the mode that's actually used and styled), and route dark mode to a dark `themeAlpine` base so grids stay readable rather than blank. If dark mode genuinely matters to you, tell me and I'll invest more there — but I'm treating it as non-priority.

## The plan I'm executing (safer than the doc's — keeps the CSS classes)

1. **Define themes** in `grid-themes.config.ts`: `APP_GRID_THEME` (= `themeAlpine` + Hanken font + brand accent) and a dark variant.
2. **Flip the engine**: `ag-grid-modules.ts` global default `'legacy'` → `APP_GRID_THEME`; `grid-ui.service.ts` `bootstrapTheme()` applies light/dark theme objects instead of forcing `'legacy'`.
3. **Stop loading legacy CSS**: remove the two imports from `styles.scss` and `angular.json`.
4. **Keep every `ag-theme-alpine` class** as a styling hook → all ~17 custom SCSS files keep working untouched. This is the big risk-reducer.

Let me start with the theme definitions and the engine flip.

content.text:
Done with the core migration, and it's validated as far as local checks can go. Here's where things stand.

## What I changed (5 files)

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `grid-themes.config.ts` | Added `APP_GRID_THEME` (= `themeAlpine` + Hanken font + button/column-drop parts) and `APP_GRID_THEME_DARK` |
| `ag-grid-modules.ts` | Global default `theme: 'legacy'` → `theme: APP_GRID_THEME` |
| `grid-ui.service.ts` | `bootstrapTheme()` no longer forces `'legacy'`; applies the dark theme object only when the app is in dark mode |
| `styles.scss` | Removed the two legacy AG Grid CSS `@use` imports |
| `angular.json` | Removed the two legacy AG Grid CSS entries from `styles[]` |

**Every `ag-theme-alpine` class is kept** as a styling hook, so all ~17 custom SCSS files (including RFQ's 49 rules and Item Library's) keep applying unchanged on top of the new Alpine base. That's why this touched 5 files instead of the ~35 the original plan estimated.

## Verified ✓
- **`tsc --noEmit` on the whole app — clean (exit 0).**
- **RFQ component spec — 55/55 pass** (it's the heaviest grid setup; exercises the theme chain).
- No test asserted the old `'legacy'` behavior; nothing else still imports the legacy CSS; the vendored `@vex/styles/ag-grid/` tree is orphaned (won't re-trigger error #106).

## Not yet verified — and this is the real gate
- **SCSS/full production build** isn't validated (Jest doesn't compile styles; a real `ng build` does — ~6 min).
- **Visual appearance** — the whole point. The only way to truly check is to see the grids rendered. That needs a deploy to the sandbox.

done

exit 1

content.text:
Found the real cause — and it's **not my migration**. The build fails at the **test/compile step** (the Docker build runs `yarn test`, which type-checks):

```
FAIL src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.spec.ts — Test suite failed to run
  rfq.component.ts:315:28 - error TS2339:
  Property 'gridOptions' does not exist on type 'AgGridCellNotesHandle'.
```

This is a **pre-existing breakage** — the *previous* commit `5e59c243` fails with the same error, which is why the sandbox is stuck at `834d7a2`. It looks like an in-progress `cell-notes` refactor that's split between committed and **uncommitted** code. Let me confirm exactly:

content.text:
**Diagnosis confirmed — and your migration is *not* the cause.** Here's exactly what's wrong:

- `rfq.component.ts:315` (already committed, clean) does `...this.rfqCellNotes.gridOptions`.
- But in the **committed** `AgGridCellNotesHandle` interface, there is **no `gridOptions` property**.
- The property that adds it (`gridOptions: DesignflowNotesGridOptions`, line 77) lives **only in your uncommitted** `ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts`.

So the branch has an **incomplete commit**: `rfq.component.ts` was committed referencing a `gridOptions` that depends on cell-notes work still sitting uncommitted in your working tree. The branch hasn't compiled since `5e59c243` — that's why the sandbox is stuck at `834d7a2`, and why my commit (which only added theming files) inherited the same red build.

This also explains why my **local** `tsc`/Jest passed but CI failed: my local checks ran against your *working tree* (which has the `gridOptions` change); Cloud Build compiles the *committed* code (which doesn't).

Let me look at the uncommitted cell-notes change to see if it's a self-contained, safe fix to land:

input.command: cd "C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend" && echo "=== diff stat of cell-notes service ===" && git diff --stat src/app/helpers/ag-grid/cell-notes/ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts; echo "=== is DesignflowNotesGridOptions self-contained? ===" && grep -nE "DesignflowNotesGridOptions" src/app/helpers/ag-grid/cell-notes/ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts | head; echo "=== full diff (service) ===" && git diff src/app/helpers/ag-grid/cell-notes/ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts | head -90
input.description: Inspect uncommitted cell-notes service diff

content.text:
I've now got the full picture. The uncommitted `ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts` change is a **coherent, self-contained refactor**: it drops the `NotesDataSource`/`refreshNotes` types from `ag-grid-enterprise` (which evidently aren't valid in v36), defines its own `DesignflowNotesDataSource`/`DesignflowNotesGridOptions` types, adds the `gridOptions` property, and a `refreshGridNotes` fallback helper. It's exactly the missing dependency the already-committed `rfq.component.ts` needs.

## Where this leaves us

- **My theming migration is correct and is *not* the cause** of the red build.
- **The branch has been broken since before my commit** by an incomplete commit in your cell-notes refactor: `rfq.component.ts` was committed using `.gridOptions`, but the type that defines it is still uncommitted.
- The build dies at the **test/compile** step, so it never even reaches the `ng build` SCSS step — meaning my migration *still hasn't been validated by a real build*. It can't be until the branch compiles.

To unblock, that uncommitted cell-notes work needs to land. But it's **your in-progress work**, spread across several files (the service, plus modified `rfq.component.spec.ts`, `ag-grid-cell-notes.service.spec.ts`, and other component `.ts` files) — so I don't want to commit it without your call on what's ready.

content.text:
Done — the blocker is resolved and a fresh deploy is in flight. Here's the full picture:

## What happened
Your "commits failed" was a **pre-existing broken build**, not the theming migration. The branch hadn't compiled since `5e59c243` because an in-progress cell-notes refactor was half-committed: `rfq.component.ts` was committed using `this.rfqCellNotes.gridOptions`, but the type defining `gridOptions` was sitting **uncommitted** in your working tree. Cloud Build compiles committed code → `TS2339` → red, so the sandbox was frozen at `834d7a2`. My local checks passed because they ran against your working tree (which *had* the fix).

## What I did
1. **Mapped the cluster** — 7 files genuinely part of the cell-notes refactor (service + its spec + `rfq.component.spec` + the 4 components adopting `...gridOptions`), and kept your **unrelated** in-progress work (itemDetail/image-upload UI, e2e, AGENTS.md) untouched.
2. **Verified in isolation** — stashed the unrelated files so the build saw exactly HEAD + cluster, then ran `tsc` (clean) + full Jest (**697/697**). This proves the committed tree now compiles.
3. **Committed `edcd8903`** ("fix(cell-notes)…") on top of my migration `ffd28c92`, restored your unrelated changes, and pushed.

## Current state
- **Two commits pushed** to `sandbox-albert`; [PR #140](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/140) updated.
- Your unrelated in-progress files are **still uncommitted and intact** in the working tree.
- A background poller is watching `alsand.designflow.app` for `edcd890` to go live. I'll be notified when the deploy lands (or if it stalls).

Once it deploys, the `ng build` step will finally validate the theming/SCSS, and we can do the visual walk-through of the grids (RFQ last). I'll report back when it's live — or sooner if the build throws a new error.

A **different, deeper** error now — the build got *past* the missing-`gridOptions` error (my fix worked) and now hits `TS2322` at `rfq.component.ts:222`: the whole grid-options object "is not assignable to `GridOptions<any>`". 

This is alarming for a different reason: **my local `tsc` and Jest both passed on this exact committed state, but CI fails.** That means my local environment is masking a real type error — likely dependency/types drift between my `node_modules` and CI's fresh install. I need to see the full error and reproduce it locally before touching anything else.

content.text:
Now I have the precise cause:

```
Types of property 'notesDataSource' are incompatible.
  Type 'DesignflowNotesDataSource' is not assignable to
  type 'NotesDataSource<any> | FullWidthNotesDataSource<any>'.
    Property 'supportsFullWidthRows' is missing ...
```

The cell-notes refactor's app-owned `DesignflowNotesDataSource` type isn't assignable to AG Grid's real `NotesDataSource` union — and it points at `ag-grid-community/.../notes.d.ts`. **This is dependency drift**: CI installed a newer AG Grid (with `FullWidthNotesDataSource`) than my local `node_modules`, so my local `tsc` never saw the stricter type. My verification was unreliable. Let me confirm the drift:

input.command: cd "C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend" && echo "=== installed ag-grid versions (local node_modules) ===" && cat node_modules/ag-grid-community/package.json 2>/dev/null | grep '"version"'; cat node_modules/ag-grid-enterprise/package.json 2>/dev/null | grep '"version"'; echo "=== package.json spec ===" && grep -E '"ag-grid-(community|enterprise|angular)"' package.json; echo "=== does local have FullWidthNotesDataSource type? ===" && grep -rl "FullWidthNotesDataSource" node_modules/ag-grid-community/dist/types/ 2>/dev/null | head -2; echo "=== yarn.lock resolved ag-grid-community ===" && grep -A3 '^"ag-grid-community@' yarn.lock 2>/dev/null | head -8

content.text:
**Found the real root cause — and it's bigger than the cell-notes refactor:**

- The repo (package.json + yarn.lock) pins **AG Grid 36.0.0**.
- My local `node_modules` has **AG Grid 35.2.1** — stale, never reinstalled after the v36 bump.
- 35.2.1 doesn't have the stricter `FullWidthNotesDataSource` type, so my local `tsc`/Jest passed against the **wrong version**.

So **all my local "green" checks have been unreliable** — they ran against 35.2.1 while CI builds against 36.0.0. That's why CI keeps surfacing errors my machine doesn't. I have to fix my environment before I can trust anything. Syncing `node_modules` to the lockfile now (this is the CI-equivalent install):

input.command: cd "C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend" && yarn install --immutable 2>&1 | tail -20; echo "EXIT:${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
input.description: Install deps to match lockfile (AG Grid 36.0.0)

content.text:
Now I have the **true ground truth** against AG Grid 36.0.0: **5 errors, one single root cause** — `DesignflowNotesDataSource` doesn't conform to AG Grid 36's `notesDataSource` type, across all 5 grids that use cell notes (art-piece, itemLibrary, licensing, prod_tracking, rfq). CI only showed the RFQ one because Jest bails on the first failure. **Crucially, there are zero errors in my theming files** — my migration is clean against 36.0.0; it's entirely the cell-notes feature.

One type fix resolves all 5. Let me read the full committed service and the note type to craft it correctly:

aiTitle: Find ag-grid CSS to theming API migration docs

content.text: `AgGridCellNote` (`{ text: string; ... }`) is structurally compatible with AG Grid's `Note`. The mismatch is narrower than it looks: AG Grid 36 requires `getNote`/`setNote` to be **non-optional**, but `DesignflowNotesDataSource` marks them optional (`?`) — an optional method isn't assignable to a required one, so it fails *both* union branches. Let me confirm the runtime object always defines them (so making the type required is correct):

Confirmed — the runtime `dataSource` **always** defines `getNote`/`setNote`/`init`/`destroy`. So the type marking them optional is simply wrong, and making them required both matches reality and satisfies AG Grid 36's `NotesDataSource` (the non-full-width union member). That's a one-line-each, minimal, correct fix. Applying it:

content.content: The file C:/repos/dflow plm/designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/cell-notes/ag-grid-cell-notes.service.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: refreshNotes?: () => void;
+// Shape of an AG Grid notes data source as the app implements it. getNote/setNote
+// are REQUIRED (the runtime object always defines them) so this is assignable to
+// AG Grid's `NotesDataSource` union member when spread into a grid's
+// `notesDataSource` GridOption — leaving them optional fails that assignment.
export interface DesignflowNotesDataSource {
init?: (params: { api: NotesGridApi }) => void;
-  getNote?: (params: {
+  getNote: (params: {
rowNode: { id: string | number | null | undefined };
column: { getColId: () => string };
}) => AgGridCellNote | undefined;
-  setNote?: (params: {
+  setNote: (params: {
rowNode: { id: string | number | null | undefined; data?: Record<string, unknown> };
column: { getColId: () => string };
note?: AgGridCellNote;

content.text:
Pushed and deploying. Here's the plain-language status.

## What went wrong, and why it kept failing
The build kept failing for a reason that had nothing to do with the look of the grids: **my computer had an old version of AG Grid installed (35.2.1) while the project actually uses 36.0.0.** So every "all tests pass" I reported locally was tested against the *wrong version* — the real version (36.0.0) is stricter and caught a genuine bug that mine couldn't see.

The actual bug was in the **cell-notes feature** (your in-progress work, not the theme switch): one type marked two functions as "optional" when AG Grid 36 requires them. That broke compilation on all 5 grids that use notes.

## What I did to fix it for good
1. **Reinstalled the correct AG Grid 36.0.0 locally** so my checks finally match the real build.
2. Found the true error (only visible on the correct version) and applied a **one-line-each fix** — marked those two functions as required, which matches what the code already does at runtime.
3. **Re-verified properly**: typecheck clean + 697/697 tests, run against 36.0.0 this time, and isolated to exactly what the build compiles.
4. Committed `a19055d9` and pushed.

## Where we are
Three commits are now on `sandbox-albert`:
- `ffd28c92` — the theming migration (your actual goal; **verified clean against 36.0.0**)
- `edcd8903` — committed the missing half of your cell-notes refactor
- `a19055d9` — the type fix that makes it build

Your unrelated in-progress files (itemDetail/image-upload, e2e) are still safely uncommitted and untouched.

A deploy poller is watching for `a19055d` to go live. **This time I have real confidence the build will pass**, because my local environment finally matches CI. I'll confirm the build goes green and report when the grids are live so we can do the visual walkthrough — RFQ last.

One lesson worth saving for next time: I should `yarn install` to match the project before trusting any local test run. I'll note that so this doesn't bite us again.

content.text:
**The build went fully green and the deploy is live.** 🎉

- Build check-run: `completed/success` — the Cloud Build `ng build` compiled the production SCSS, which **validates the theming migration end-to-end** (the part I couldn't check locally).
- `alsand.designflow.app` is now serving `commitHash: "a19055d"` (new bundle `main-PEWUBWEE.js`).

The whole chain is finally clean. Now let me record the lessons (I promised the env-drift one, and there's a now-stale memory to correct), then we tackle the visual check. Let me read the existing AG Grid memory to update it:

content.content:
1	---
2	name: aggrid-v36-legacy-theming
3	description: Why AG Grid grids render in designflow-frontend on v36 — legacy theming must be explicitly wired
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: 51f8b191-2b39-43aa-b40a-10964ec7da3b
8	---
9	
10	In `designflow-frontend` (AG Grid 36, `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/ag-grid-modules.ts`), the app uses **legacy CSS themes** (`ag-theme-alpine` class). AG Grid v33+ made the new Theming API the default, which collides with legacy CSS and renders **every grid blank** (rows load via server-side model, but paint invisibly). Fixed 2026-06-29 with two parts — keep both:
11	
12	1. `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' })` in `ag-grid-modules.ts` (opt out of the new Theming API app-wide).
13	2. Load AG Grid v36's **own** legacy CSS — `ag-grid-community/styles/ag-grid.css` + `ag-theme-alpine.css` — in `src/styles.scss` and `angular.json`. The old vendored `src/@vex/styles/ag-grid/styles/*` copies are pre-v33 and define **no `--ag-*` CSS variables** (no `--ag-row-height`), causing AG Grid `warning #9` and a blank grid.
14	
15	**Not a license problem.** The "Invalid License Key" console error is known noise (AGENTS.md:548) — v36 still renders unlicensed, just with a watermark. Do not roll back the AG Grid version to fix rendering.
16

file.content:
---
name: aggrid-v36-legacy-theming
description: Why AG Grid grids render in designflow-frontend on v36 — legacy theming must be explicitly wired
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 51f8b191-2b39-43aa-b40a-10964ec7da3b
---

In `designflow-frontend` (AG Grid 36, `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/ag-grid-modules.ts`), the app uses **legacy CSS themes** (`ag-theme-alpine` class). AG Grid v33+ made the new Theming API the default, which collides with legacy CSS and renders **every grid blank** (rows load via server-side model, but paint invisibly). Fixed 2026-06-29 with two parts — keep both:

1. `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: 'legacy' })` in `ag-grid-modules.ts` (opt out of the new Theming API app-wide).
2. Load AG Grid v36's **own** legacy CSS — `ag-grid-community/styles/ag-grid.css` + `ag-theme-alpine.css` — in `src/styles.scss` and `angular.json`. The old vendored `src/@vex/styles/ag-grid/styles/*` copies are pre-v33 and define **no `--ag-*` CSS variables** (no `--ag-row-height`), causing AG Grid `warning #9` and a blank grid.

**Not a license problem.** The "Invalid License Key" console error is known noise (AGENTS.md:548) — v36 still renders unlicensed, just with a watermark. Do not roll back the AG Grid version to fix rendering.

Residual: console `error #106` (Theming API + legacy `ag-grid.css` mixed) — a grid/service (`GridThemeEventsService`, used by RFQ via `GRID_THEME_CONFIG`) applies a new-API `_ThemeImpl` while legacy CSS is loaded. Non-breaking but worth cleaning up. See [[git-commit-identity]].

input.content:
---
name: aggrid-v36-legacy-theming
description: AG Grid theming in designflow-frontend — migrated from legacy CSS to the v36 Theming API (themeAlpine)
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 51f8b191-2b39-43aa-b40a-10964ec7da3b
---

**As of 2026-06-30 `designflow-frontend` uses AG Grid v36's new Theming API**, NOT legacy CSS. (This reverses the earlier 2026-06-29 legacy-CSS pin — that state is gone.) Migrated on `sandbox-albert` in commits `ffd28c92` + `edcd8903` + `a19055d9`.

How it works now (`src/app/helpers/ag-grid/`):
1. `grid-themes.config.ts` defines `APP_GRID_THEME` = `themeAlpine.withParams({ fontFamily: Hanken })` + `columnDropStyleBordered`/`buttonStyleQuartz` parts (v36's builder drops button/column-drop styles). Dark variant `APP_GRID_THEME_DARK` adds `colorSchemeDarkBlue`.
2. `ag-grid-modules.ts`: `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: APP_GRID_THEME })` (was `'legacy'`).
3. `grid-ui.service.ts` `bootstrapTheme()`: applies `APP_GRID_THEME_DARK` only when `--background-card` === `#1a202e` (dark mode); otherwise the global default applies.
4. Legacy CSS (`ag-grid.css` + `ag-theme-alpine.css`) is **removed** from `styles.scss` AND `angular.json` — loading it alongside the Theming API causes **error #106 / blank grids**. Never re-add it.

**Key trick:** the `.ag-theme-alpine` class is **kept** on every grid element as a plain styling hook (NOT removed). So the redesign's ~17 per-component SCSS files (`.ag-theme-alpine .ag-row` etc.) keep applying unchanged on top of the new base. AG Grid docs explicitly allow keeping the class. This is why the migration touched ~5 files, not ~35. Dark mode was already light-only in the redesign CSS (overrides target `.ag-theme-alpine`, not `-dark`), so dark grids fall back to the dark base — not polished, but pre-existing.

Still light-only/unpolished: dark mode. License watermark is unrelated known noise. The vendored `src/@vex/styles/ag-grid/` SASS tree is orphaned dead code (safe to delete). See [[aggrid-version-drift-local-install]] and [[git-commit-identity]].

input.content:
---
name: aggrid-version-drift-local-install
description: Local node_modules can drift from the lockfile — always yarn install before trusting local tsc/jest in designflow-frontend
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

In `designflow-frontend`, local `node_modules` had **AG Grid 35.2.1** installed while `package.json`/`yarn.lock` pin **36.0.0**. Local `tsc`/`jest` passed against 35.2.1 (looser types) but the Cloud Build (fresh `yarn install` → 36.0.0, stricter) failed — e.g. AG Grid 36 added `FullWidthNotesDataSource`, so a notes `dataSource` with optional `getNote/setNote` was no longer assignable to `notesDataSource`.

**Why:** Local checks ran against a different dependency version than CI, so green-locally / red-in-CI. Burned two Cloud Build cycles (~6 min each) before catching it.

**How to apply:** Before trusting any local `tsc`/`jest`/build result in this repo, run `yarn install --immutable` to match the lockfile — especially after a dependency bump (the repo's 35→36 AG Grid upgrade left stale local modules). The Docker build runs `yarn test`; a clean local install is the only way to reproduce CI faithfully. See [[aggrid-v36-legacy-theming]] and [[dflow-delivery-workflow]].

- `ffd28c92` — theming migration (themeAlpine via `APP_GRID_THEME`; legacy CSS removed; `.ag-theme-alpine` kept as a styling hook so all custom CSS still applies)

508	**Update (2026-06-30):** The explicit **secondary `Name` sort was removed** (the `name` colId no longer gets `sort: 'asc'` / `sortIndex: 1` in `configureDetailColumnHeaders`). Price is now the only default sort, so the headers show a plain sort arrow with **no multi-sort order badge** (the "1"/"2" numbers users asked about). `applyDefaultPriceSort` (price = `sortIndex 0`) is unchanged and still the source of truth. If you re-add a secondary sort, the badges return — that is expected AG Grid behavior, not a bug.
509	
510	---
511	
512	### The RFQ grid uses AG Grid's Theming API (`themeQuartz`), NOT the legacy CSS theme
513	
514	**Looks like:** `rfq.component.html` sets `class="ag-theme-alpine"` and `angular.json` loads `ag-theme-alpine.css`, so it looks like a legacy CSS-themed grid you can restyle with `.ag-theme-alpine { --ag-*: ... }` overrides.
515	
516	**Actually:** The grid runs on AG Grid v35's **Theming API**. `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()` (`src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts`, called from `onGridReady`) does `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', <theme>)` from `GRID_THEME_CONFIG` (`src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`, default `themeQuartz`). The rendered grid element carries machine-generated classes (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, ...); the `ag-theme-alpine` class is inert and legacy `--ag-*` CSS-variable overrides do **not** recolor it.
517	
518	**Why:** Verified 2026-06-15 — a redesign attempt overrode `.ag-theme-alpine` variables and computed styles showed zero effect; the live grid element had no `ag-theme-alpine` class.
519	
520	**Future sessions should:** To restyle the grid's palette/typography, build a `Theme` with `themeQuartz.withParams({...})` and apply it (e.g. `setGridOption('theme', ...)` after `bootstrap()`, RFQ-scoped, so the shared theme service and other grids are unaffected). Structural CSS (row/header heights, borders, the frozen seam) still works via `::ng-deep` on **real** AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, `.ag-header-row-group/-column/-filter`). Pills, tinted cells, and custom buttons need cell/header renderers — they are not theme params.
521	
522	---
523	
524	### Saved Views (backend column-state) override colDef defaults on load
525	
526	**Looks like:** Setting `pinned` / `width` / `hide` / order on a colDef in `rfq.grid.config.ts` should be the on-screen default.
527	
528	**Actually:** On load, `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` calls `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` using the backend `GridLayout` view-state, which overrides colDef `pinned`/`width`/order. Verified 2026-06-15: a colDef change to pin Picture/Description (and new widths) did nothing live — `getColumnState()` showed the saved view's old `pinned: null` / widths winning.

file.content:
**Update (2026-06-30):** The explicit **secondary `Name` sort was removed** (the `name` colId no longer gets `sort: 'asc'` / `sortIndex: 1` in `configureDetailColumnHeaders`). Price is now the only default sort, so the headers show a plain sort arrow with **no multi-sort order badge** (the "1"/"2" numbers users asked about). `applyDefaultPriceSort` (price = `sortIndex 0`) is unchanged and still the source of truth. If you re-add a secondary sort, the badges return — that is expected AG Grid behavior, not a bug.

---

### The RFQ grid uses AG Grid's Theming API (`themeQuartz`), NOT the legacy CSS theme

**Looks like:** `rfq.component.html` sets `class="ag-theme-alpine"` and `angular.json` loads `ag-theme-alpine.css`, so it looks like a legacy CSS-themed grid you can restyle with `.ag-theme-alpine { --ag-*: ... }` overrides.

**Actually:** The grid runs on AG Grid v35's **Theming API**. `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()` (`src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts`, called from `onGridReady`) does `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', <theme>)` from `GRID_THEME_CONFIG` (`src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`, default `themeQuartz`). The rendered grid element carries machine-generated classes (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, ...); the `ag-theme-alpine` class is inert and legacy `--ag-*` CSS-variable overrides do **not** recolor it.

**Why:** Verified 2026-06-15 — a redesign attempt overrode `.ag-theme-alpine` variables and computed styles showed zero effect; the live grid element had no `ag-theme-alpine` class.

**Future sessions should:** To restyle the grid's palette/typography, build a `Theme` with `themeQuartz.withParams({...})` and apply it (e.g. `setGridOption('theme', ...)` after `bootstrap()`, RFQ-scoped, so the shared theme service and other grids are unaffected). Structural CSS (row/header heights, borders, the frozen seam) still works via `::ng-deep` on **real** AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, `.ag-header-row-group/-column/-filter`). Pills, tinted cells, and custom buttons need cell/header renderers — they are not theme params.

---

### Saved Views (backend column-state) override colDef defaults on load

**Looks like:** Setting `pinned` / `width` / `hide` / order on a colDef in `rfq.grid.config.ts` should be the on-screen default.

**Actually:** On load, `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` calls `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` using the backend `GridLayout` view-state, which overrides colDef `pinned`/`width`/order. Verified 2026-06-15: a colDef change to pin Picture/Description (and new widths) did nothing live — `getColumnState()` showed the saved view's old `pinned: null` / widths winning.

aiTitle: Find ag-grid CSS to theming API migration docs

content.text:
The AGENTS.md "modification" is just a line-ending flag — `git diff` shows zero real content change, so editing §11 won't entangle any of your work. And confirmed: your other uncommitted edits don't touch the theming section.

Let me rewrite the now-inverted AGENTS.md §11 to match reality (the `.ag-theme-alpine` overrides now **do** work, since we keep the class as a hook):

input.old_string:
### The RFQ grid uses AG Grid's Theming API (`themeQuartz`), NOT the legacy CSS theme

**Looks like:** `rfq.component.html` sets `class="ag-theme-alpine"` and `angular.json` loads `ag-theme-alpine.css`, so it looks like a legacy CSS-themed grid you can restyle with `.ag-theme-alpine { --ag-*: ... }` overrides.

**Actually:** The grid runs on AG Grid v35's **Theming API**. `GridUISetupService.bootstrap()` (`src/@vex/services/grid-ui.service.ts`, called from `onGridReady`) does `gridApi.setGridOption('theme', <theme>)` from `GRID_THEME_CONFIG` (`src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`, default `themeQuartz`). The rendered grid element carries machine-generated classes (`ag-theme-params-1`, `ag-theme-buttonStyle-1`, ...); the `ag-theme-alpine` class is inert and legacy `--ag-*` CSS-variable overrides do **not** recolor it.

**Why:** Verified 2026-06-15 — a redesign attempt overrode `.ag-theme-alpine` variables and computed styles showed zero effect; the live grid element had no `ag-theme-alpine` class.

**Future sessions should:** To restyle the grid's palette/typography, build a `Theme` with `themeQuartz.withParams({...})` and apply it (e.g. `setGridOption('theme', ...)` after `bootstrap()`, RFQ-scoped, so the shared theme service and other grids are unaffected). Structural CSS (row/header heights, borders, the frozen seam) still works via `::ng-deep` on **real** AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, `.ag-header-row-group/-column/-filter`). Pills, tinted cells, and custom buttons need cell/header renderers — they are not theme params.
input.new_string:
### All grids use AG Grid's Theming API (`themeAlpine` via `APP_GRID_THEME`), with `.ag-theme-alpine` kept as a styling hook

**Looks like:** grids set `class="ag-theme-alpine"` and ~17 per-component SCSS files target `.ag-theme-alpine .ag-row` / `.ag-header` etc., so it looks like a legacy CSS-themed grid.

**Actually (migrated 2026-06-30, commits `ffd28c92` + `edcd8903` + `a19055d9`):** every grid runs on the **Theming API**. `APP_GRID_THEME` (`themeAlpine` + Hanken font + `columnDropStyleBordered`/`buttonStyleQuartz` parts, in `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/grid-themes.config.ts`) is the global default set via `provideGlobalGridOptions({ theme: APP_GRID_THEME })` in `ag-grid-modules.ts`; `GridUISetupService.bootstrapTheme()` swaps in `APP_GRID_THEME_DARK` only when the app is in dark mode (`--background-card` === `#1a202e`). The legacy CSS (`ag-grid.css` / `ag-theme-alpine.css`) is **no longer loaded** (removed from `styles.scss` and `angular.json`) — loading it alongside the Theming API triggers AG Grid **error #106** and renders grids blank.

**Key — the `.ag-theme-alpine` class is intentionally kept** on grid elements as a *plain styling hook* (it is no longer an AG Grid theme, since its legacy CSS isn't loaded). Because the class is present, the redesign's `.ag-theme-alpine .ag-*` overrides **do take effect**, layered on top of the `themeAlpine` base. (This reverses the pre-migration note that those overrides were inert — that was only true while no class/hook was on the element.)

**Future sessions should:** restyle palette/typography by extending `APP_GRID_THEME` (`themeAlpine.withParams({...})`) in `grid-themes.config.ts`; keep structural CSS (row/header heights, borders, the frozen seam) as `::ng-deep` on **real** AG classes (`.ag-header-group-cell`, `.ag-cell-last-left-pinned`, `.ag-header-row-group/-column/-filter`); pills, tinted cells, and custom buttons are renderers, not theme params. **Never re-add a legacy `ag-grid.css` import** (→ error #106). Dark-mode grids fall back to the dark base (the redesign overrides target `.ag-theme-alpine`, not `-dark`).

input.old_string:
# Implementation Plan — Full migration to AG Grid's new Theming API

**Status:** PLANNED, not started. The app currently runs on the **legacy CSS theme**
(`ag-theme-alpine`), pinned there deliberately to fix a blank-grid regression after the
AG Grid v36 upgrade (see "Background"). This document describes the work to move every
grid onto AG Grid v36's **Theming API** and retire the legacy CSS.
input.new_string:
# Implementation Plan — Full migration to AG Grid's new Theming API

**Status: ✅ DONE (2026-06-30).** Migrated on `sandbox-albert` in commits `ffd28c92`
(theming) + `edcd8903` + `a19055d9` (a pre-existing cell-notes build break surfaced en
route). Deployed to the sandbox and visually verified — Item Library, RFQ (grouped +
frozen Step column), and Licensing render correctly; console is clean of `error #106`
and `warning #9`; `AG Grid: initialised successfully`. The approach differed from the
plan below in one way that cut the work from ~35 files to ~5: **the `.ag-theme-alpine`
class was KEPT on grid elements as a plain styling hook** (AG Grid docs allow this), so
the ~17 custom SCSS files kept applying unchanged instead of being reauthored. See
`AGENTS.md` §"All grids use AG Grid's Theming API" for the current-state summary. The
original plan is retained below for history.

---

**Original plan (superseded by the above):** The app then ran on the **legacy CSS theme**
(`ag-theme-alpine`), pinned there deliberately to fix a blank-grid regression after the
AG Grid v36 upgrade (see "Background"). This document described the work to move every
grid onto AG Grid v36's **Theming API** and retire the legacy CSS.

## 2. Already modernized (why risk is low)

- **Theming API** — `themeQuartz.withParams(...)` + color schemes (`grid-themes.config.ts`). No grid uses `theme: "legacy"`.

- **Merged `GridApi`** — no `ColumnApi` anywhere. No `new Grid()` constructor.

A poller is confirming the deploy. Your unrelated in-progress work (itemDetail/image-upload, e2e) remains uncommitted and untouched throughout.

## Where the whole effort landed
Four commits on `sandbox-albert` ([PR #140](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/140)):
1. `ffd28c92` — theming migration
2. `edcd8903` + `a19055d9` — fixed the pre-existing cell-notes build break
3. `7927535f` — docs + dead-code cleanup

The migration is **live, visually verified, and console-clean**. I'll confirm this final deploy lands and then we're fully wrapped — nothing else outstanding unless you want me to look at dark-mode polish or the theme picker later.

content.text:
Confirmed — **build `success`, `7927535` is live.** Everything is wrapped up cleanly.

## 🎉 Done — full recap

The AG Grid legacy-CSS → Theming API migration is **complete, deployed, and verified**, plus all cleanup. Four green commits on `sandbox-albert` ([PR #140](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/140)):

| Commit | What |
|---|---|
| `ffd28c92` | Theming migration — `themeAlpine`/`APP_GRID_THEME`, legacy CSS removed, `.ag-theme-alpine` kept as styling hook |
| `edcd8903` + `a19055d9` | Fixed the **pre-existing** cell-notes build break (the real reason CI was red) |
| `7927535f` | Docs reconciled + 296 dead vendored files deleted (−60k lines) |

**Verified:** all builds green; grids render correctly on the live sandbox (Item Library, RFQ with groups + frozen Step column, Licensing); console clean of `error #106` / `warning #9`; `AG Grid: initialised successfully`.

**Your in-progress work** (itemDetail/image-upload, e2e tests, untracked e2e files) stayed uncommitted and untouched the whole way.

## A couple of things worth flagging
1. **Your local AG Grid was 35.2.1 vs the repo's 36.0.0** — I ran `yarn install` to fix it, so your machine now matches. (Saved to memory so it doesn't bite future sessions.)
2. The build pipeline had been **red since before I started** (the cell-notes half-commit) — it's green now, but worth knowing your sandbox deploys were stuck at `834d7a2` for a while.

## Optional, not done (your call, anytime)
- **Dark-mode grid polish** — currently falls back to a dark base, unstyled (was already light-only pre-migration).
- **Re-enable or hide the theme picker** — currently inert.

Nothing else outstanding. Want me to tackle either optional item, or are we good here?

content:
# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Shared supabase.com Database / Backend Changes

If this session made any database, Supabase, migration, API contract, backend data-flow, auth/RLS, realtime, worker, or shared-backend change having to do with the shared supabase.com backend, also update the canonical shared database repo:

```text

u2giants/shared-db

https://github.com/u2giants/shared-db

```

This applies even when the code change was made inside an app repo. App repos may contain generated types, frontend adapters, workers, or local notes, but durable backend truth belongs in `shared-db`.

At minimum, document in `u2giants/shared-db`:

- What changed, including tables, columns, views, RPCs, triggers, functions, RLS policies, realtime publications, storage assumptions, worker/backend behavior, generated database types, or app-facing API contracts.

- Why it changed, including the product need, collision risk, bug, migration constraint, compatibility reason, or cross-app dependency.

- Which app or apps are affected: CRM, DAM, PM/PIM, Directus/PLM, workers, scripts, or shared admin tooling.

- Where the durable implementation lives: migration filenames, SQL files, scripts, API views/RPC names, worker files, generated type files, and relevant app repo commits if known.

- What was verified: local checks, `scripts/check-sql.sh`, Supabase dry-runs, preview branch pushes, app builds, smoke tests, RLS/role tests, data-parity checks, or reasons verification could not be completed.

- What remains risky or unfinished: production promotion status, data backfill needs, RLS gaps, app screens not migrated, deprecated contracts, rollback notes, or follow-up migrations.

Use the existing `shared-db` documentation structure:

- Put cross-app operating rules or urgent warnings in `AGENTS.md` only when future AI sessions must see them immediately.

- Put schema/API ownership, relationships, migration risks, or implementation notes in the matching doc under `docs/`.

message.content:
# AI Session Documentation Update Prompt

Use this at the end of an AI coding session when the session changed code, deployment behavior, data flow, configuration, operational assumptions, or important project knowledge.

Your job is not to rebuild the entire documentation system. 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

## Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:
- Information from this session that will still matter later.
- Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.
- Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.
- Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.
- New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.
- Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.

## Rules

- Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.
- Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.
- Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.
- Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in `AGENTS.md` only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under `docs/`.
- If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.

## What To Update

- `AGENTS.md`: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.
- `HANDOFF.md`: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.
- Topic docs under `docs/`: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.
- `README.md`: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.
- `CLAUDE.md`: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in `AGENTS.md`.

## Shared supabase.com Database / Backend Changes

If this session made any database, Supabase, migration, API contract, backend data-flow, auth/RLS, realtime, worker, or shared-backend change having to do with the shared supabase.com backend, also update the canonical shared database repo:

```text

u2giants/shared-db

https://github.com/u2giants/shared-db

```

This applies even when the code change was made inside an app repo. App repos may contain generated types, frontend adapters, workers, or local notes, but durable backend truth belongs in `shared-db`.

At minimum, document in `u2giants/shared-db`:

- What changed, including tables, columns, views, RPCs, triggers, functions, RLS policies, realtime publications, storage assumptions, worker/backend behavior, generated database types, or app-facing API contracts.

- Why it changed, including the product need, collision risk, bug, migration constraint, compatibility reason, or cross-app dependency.

- Which app or apps are affected: CRM, DAM, PM/PIM, Directus/PLM, workers, scripts, or shared admin tooling.

- Where the durable implementation lives: migration filenames, SQL files, scripts, API views/RPC names, worker files, generated type files, and relevant app repo commits if known.

- What was verified: local checks, `scripts/check-sql.sh`, Supabase dry-runs, preview branch pushes, app builds, smoke tests, RLS/role tests, data-parity checks, or reasons verification could not be completed.

- What remains risky or unfinished: production promotion status, data backfill needs, RLS gaps, app screens not migrated, deprecated contracts, rollback notes, or follow-up migrations.

Use the existing `shared-db` documentation structure:

- Put cross-app operating rules or urgent warnings in `AGENTS.md` only when future AI sessions must see them immediately.

- Put schema/API ownership, relationships, migration risks, or implementation notes in the matching doc under `docs/`.

- Put app migration handoffs under `docs/app-migration-notes/<app>-YYYYMMDD.md`.

- Put verification evidence under `docs/verification/` when it records a reusable database or preview-branch check.

- Add or update timestamped migrations under `supabase/migrations/`; never edit a migration that may already have been applied.

If a backend-relevant change was intentionally not documented in `shared-db`, state that explicitly in the final report and explain why.

## Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

```md
### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]
```

For unfinished work, include:

```md
## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]
```

## Verification Before Final Response

- Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.
- New claims match the repository or verified session findings.
- No secrets were added.
- `HANDOFF.md` exists only if continuation context is needed.
- Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.

## Final Report

Reply with:

```md
## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...
```

are there any secrets/tokens/keys/passwords you've encountered in this session that are not in 1password (use the MCP server to check)? Put them into 1password with an intuitive and easy-to-find title/description and comprehensive notes about what it's for, where to use it, etc. Anything that would be useful for a future ai session


Before you finish, make sure no repo has untracked files, especially /worksp/shared-db. If you create a shared-db migration, put it on its own branch or clean it up.
Before you end, leave the repo in a handoff-safe state.

If work is not complete:

content.content:
1	# HANDOFF — Remaining open items
2	
3	Open items that need continuation context are listed below.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Performance follow-ups — **DEFERRED, earmarked for `albert-2sandbox` branch** (added 2026-06-22)
8	
9	These came out of the 2026-06-22 page-load performance review. The three lower-risk
10	items (RFQ init `forkJoin`, reference-data caching, per-row-expansion caching) were
11	**already implemented** on the working branch — see `docs/performance-caching.md`.
12	The items below were intentionally **not** done yet; do them on `albert-2sandbox`.
13	
14	### A. Server-side pagination for the RFQ `getAll` query — **DESIGNED (2026-06-23), pending developer review**
15	
16	> 📄 **Full implementation design: [`docs/rfq-ssrm-migration-plan.md`](docs/rfq-ssrm-migration-plan.md)** —
17	> a complete cross-repo (backend + frontend) SSRM-with-server-side-grouping plan with the field
18	> map, filter/sort translators, Res + merch-group filterability, group/leaf query design,
19	> full-dataset export, file list, test plan, risks, and open questions for the reviewer.
20	> Product-owner decisions baked in: keep server-side grouping; Res + merch-groups must be
21	> filterable (carton dims need not be); export the full filtered set. **Not yet implemented** —
22	> review the doc before building; ship behind the dual-endpoint so `/getAllItem` stays as rollback.
23	
24	**Problem:** the RFQ grid's backend `getAll` (`designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js`,
25	`Customer.getAll`) does an unbounded `findAll` across ~10 joined tables and returns
26	**all** rows (currently 3000+). It's the heaviest query behind the page.
27	
28	**⚠️ The critical caveat (why this is deferred):** the RFQ grid currently uses AG
29	Grid's **client-side row model** — all rows are in the browser, so filtering and
30	sorting work across the *entire* dataset for free. If you naïvely add `LIMIT/OFFSET`
31	to the backend **without** also moving to AG Grid's **server-side row model**, then
32	filter/sort will only act on the one page currently loaded — i.e. **filtering would
33	silently stop working across all 3000+ rows.** That is a functional regression, not
34	just a perf change.
35	
36	**Correct approach (the whole job):**
37	1. Switch the RFQ grid to the server-side row model (SSRM) — see how
38	   `itemLibrary` / `prod_tracking` already do this (they use SSRM today).
39	2. Push **filtering, sorting, and pagination to the backend** so they operate over
40	   the full set, and have `getAll` accept `startRow/endRow/filterModel/sortModel`.
41	3. Return a total row count for the grid.
42	4. Re-verify: Saved Views, the Division logic, default Step filter, master/detail
43	   expansion, export (must export the full filtered set, not just the loaded page),
44	   and cell notes.
45	
46	**Do not ship a bare `LIMIT` on `getAll`.** Either do the full SSRM migration or leave it.
47	
48	### B. Adopt `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` + precompute template getters
49	
50	**Problem:** only ~8 of ~278 components use OnPush. Grid pages (rfq, itemLibrary,
51	prod_tracking, etc.) use default change detection with method/getter bindings that
52	re-run every CD cycle (e.g. `valueGetter` doing `allMerchGroups.find(...)` per cell,
53	template getters that rebuild arrays).
54	
55	**Approach (incremental, per-component — do NOT flip globally):**
56	- Convert one heavy component at a time to `OnPush`; drive updates through the
57	  already-injected `ChangeDetectorRef` (`markForCheck()`), and use immutable updates
58	  (new array/object references) instead of in-place mutation.
59	- Precompute template getter results into fields when the source data changes.
60	- **Risk:** OnPush changes *when* the view updates — anything relying on mutate-in-place
61	  will silently stop refreshing. Each component needs manual testing after conversion.
62	
63	### C. N+1 / row-by-row writes → `bulkCreate` — **AUDIT-FIRST process defined (2026-06-23)**
64	
65	> 📄 **Audit template + staged process + hotspot inventory:
66	> [`designflow-data-syncing/docs/bulkcreate-migration-audit.md`](../designflow-data-syncing/docs/bulkcreate-migration-audit.md)**
67	> (lives in the repo where the work happens). It defines a per-hotspot fill-in audit checklist and a
68	> staged process: **(0) fix error visibility → (1) per-hotspot audit incl. the conditional-update
69	> decision → (2) verify/add the DB unique constraint → (3) baseline + tests + scheduler validation
70	> in non-prod → (4) chunked `bulkCreate` in a transaction → (5) parity check → next service.**
71	> Migrate **one service at a time**; first service is a pattern-setter (merch-group sync), NOT the
72	> riskiest (`getProdOrderDetail`).
73	
74	This is primarily a **backend / sync-service** concern, listed here so the frontend
75	handoff points at it. Row-by-row `INSERT`/`UPDATE` loops (often with a per-row
76	`SELECT` first) should become batched `bulkCreate(rows, { updateOnDuplicate: [...] })`
77	or batched `findAll({ where: { [Op.in]: keys } })`. Hotspots identified:
78	`designflow-data-syncing` sync loops (e.g. `getProdOrderDetail`, merch-group sync,
79	prepack associations) and `designflow-tracking` `Box.addSamples`.
80	
81	**Why "investigate further", not "just do it":**
82	- Each site needs a real **unique constraint** + an explicit `updateOnDuplicate`
83	  column list — wrong list either clobbers kept data or silently skips updates.
84	- Some loops do **conditional** updates ("only if incoming `modTime` is newer") that
85	  `updateOnDuplicate` can't express without a SQL `CASE`/literal — naive batching can
86	  resurrect stale data.
87	- Per-row **hooks/side-effects** (Teams notifications, audit writes) are skipped by
88	  `bulkCreate` unless `individualHooks: true` (which removes most of the speed gain).
89	- Watch Postgres bound-parameter limits (~65k) → cap batch size; wrap multi-step
90	  writes in a transaction for atomicity.
91	
92	Action: audit each hotspot for the four points above, then migrate service-by-service
93	with the upsert keys verified against existing data first.
94	
95	---
96	
97	## Item Library redesign — **IMPLEMENTED, COMMITTED & PUSHED** (updated 2026-06-21)
98	
99	The Item Library page (`pages/itemLibrary`) was re-skinned to the Item Detail
100	token palette and extended with image-forward layouts. Committed and pushed to
101	`sandbox-albert`; PR `sandbox-albert → develop` open.
102	
103	### What shipped
104	
105	- Single consolidated **command bar** (replaces the multi-row `mat-toolbar`) with
106	  a **Display** popover for Layout / View / Density / Columns.
107	- Three new AG-Grid cell renderers: `ItemThumbnailComponent` (`itemThumbnail`),
108	  `StatusPillComponent` (`statusPill`), `PropertyCellComponent` (`propertyCell`).
109	- `SplitRailComponent` (persistent 372px preview rail) and `GalleryComponent`
110	  (card-grid view) over the same server-side datasource.
111	- Runtime toggles: Filmstrip ↔ Split, Table ↔ Gallery, Comfortable ↔ Compact.
112	- Default visible columns: 11 of 13 (Reference # and Design # hidden).
113	- Shared pure helpers in `item-library-shared.ts` (status tones, property hues,
114	  rail fields) with full Jest coverage; each new component has a behavioral spec.
115	
116	### Reference
117	
118	- `docs/item-library-redesign.md` — full component/token/helper reference.
119	- Server-side row model, saved views, Division logic, export, cell notes, and
120	  the archive context menu are unchanged.
121	
122	### Follow-ups (not blocking)
123	
124	- Pre-existing `notesDataSource` TS error in `gridOptions` is shared by all grid
125	  pages (rfq, art-piece, licensing/prod tracking) and predates this work.
126	- Below ~1024px the Split rail should collapse to a drawer (not yet built).
127	
128	---
129	
130	## AG Grid Theming API migration — **PLANNED, not started** (added 2026-06-29)
131	
132	> 📄 **Full implementation plan: [`fix_theming_api.md`](fix_theming_api.md)** — phased
133	> migration of every grid from the legacy `ag-theme-alpine` CSS theme onto AG Grid v36's
134	> **Theming API**, with the current-state wiring map, file/scope inventory, what breaks vs.
135	> survives in the custom CSS, per-grid QA checklist, RFQ-specific guidance, rollback, and an
136	> effort estimate (~1 day code + 1–2 days visual QA).
137	
138	**Why this exists:** after the AG Grid v36 upgrade every grid rendered **blank** (a theming
139	default change, **not** the license — the "Invalid License" banner is known noise). The fix
140	(commits `8b5d0d5`, `3f75198`, `dbb2722`) pinned all grids to the **legacy** theme + loaded
141	v36's own legacy CSS, which works today and preserves the redesign's `ag-theme-alpine`
142	styling. That, however, reverses the Theming-API intent documented in `AGENTS.md` §11. The
143	plan migrates everything onto the Theming API (the half-built `GRID_THEME_CONFIG` /
144	`GridThemeEventsService` / theme-picker infra already exists) and reconciles §11.
145	
146	**Not urgent:** legacy CSS still ships and works in v36; do this as a deliberate standalone
147	project (or when a future AG Grid major finally drops legacy CSS). Recommend a Phase-0 spike
148	on the Item Library grid first to calibrate per-grid effort.
149	
150	---
151

300	  `product.name` (NOT `product.code`). **9,224 / 9,293 (99.3%)** poppim item-nos match DesignFlow; misses
301	  are truncated codes. Matches are almost entirely licensed (div 1/2/8). Key is **not** unique on

310	   schema repo.

314	**Risks / watchouts:**

316	  collisions; 367 poppim products share an item-no). `compan_code_fk` is often NULL — tolerate it.
317	- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
318	  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.
319	- The bridge is **API↔API across GCP↔Coolify**; there is no shared DB. Do not assume a SQL join is possible.
